Article type: Communication 
 
Strongly Coupled Interfaces between Carbon Host and Sulfur-Containing Guest for 
Highly-Stable Lithium-Sulfur Batteries 
 
Hong-Jie Peng, Qiang Zhang*, Jia-Qi Huang, Xin-Bing Cheng, Meng-Qing Guo, Zhe Yuan, 
Lian-Yuan He, Fei Wei 
 
H. J. Peng, Prof. Q. Zhang, Dr. J. Q. Huang, X. B. Huang, M. Q. Guo, Z. Yuan, L. Y. He, 
Prof. F. Wei. 
Beijing Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Reaction Engineering and Technology 
Department of Chemical Engineering 
Tsinghua University 
Beijing 100084, P. R. China 
E-mail: zhang-qiang@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn.  
 
Keywords: lithium-sulfur battery, interface, carbon, host-guest chemistry, cathode. 
  
 
 
 
 
  
   
 
2 
 
The high energy density storage technology is strongly considered for the widely use of 
portable electronic devices (e.g. smart phones, laptop computers, cameras), the development 
of electric vehicles, as well as the controllable energy harvest from renewable energy (e.g. 
wind energy, solar energy). Lithium–sulfur (Li-S) batteries are strongly considered due to the 
fact that elemental sulfur offers a high theoretical specific capacity (1672 mAh g-1 based on 
the electrochemical reaction 16 Li + S8 = 8 Li2S) and therefore, Li-S batteries demonstrate 
high theoretical energy density (2600 Wh kg-1), which is 3 to 5 times higher than that of 
traditional Li-ion batteries based on intercalation chemistry.[1, 2, 3] The use of sulfur as the 
cathode possesses the advantages of natural abundance, low cost, environmental benignancy 
and wide range of operating temperature. However, there are many basic obstacles to fully 
demonstrate the potential of sulfur for a Li-S cell. Both of the sulfur and lithium sulfides are 
intrinsic insulate, leading to the low utilization of active materials in the cathode and poor 
electrochemical reversibility. There is a remarkable volume change during the charge-
discharge cycle. Meanwhile, the induced high order polysulfides diffuse to the anode side, 
where they react with metallic lithium to form low order polysulfides.[2] The low order 
polysulfides diffuse back, which is described as shuttle effect that lower the efficiency. 
Consequently, the discharge capacity always decays rapidly. 
The use of conductive framework as the host scaffold to obtain a carbon/sulfur 
composite cathode is an efficient and effective way to increase the utilization of sulfur for 
redox reaction.[1, 2] The nanostructured carbon (e.g. ordered mesoporous carbon,[4, 5] 
hierarchical porous carbon,[6] carbide derived carbon,[7] carbon hollow spheres,[8] carbon 
nanotubes (CNTs),[9, 10] graphene,[11] CNT/graphene hybrid,[12] CNT/porous carbon hybrid[13]),
main_text:
DOI: 10.1002/((please add manuscript number))
Strongly Coupled Interfaces between Carbon Host and Sulfur-Containing Guest for
Highly-Stable Lithium-Sulfur Batteries
Hong-Jie Peng, Qiang Zhang*, Jia-Qi Huang, Xin-Bing Cheng, Meng-Qing Guo, Zhe Yuan,
H. J. Peng, Prof. Q. Zhang, Dr. J. Q. Huang, X. B. Huang, M. Q. Guo, Z. Yuan, L. Y. He,
Beijing Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Reaction Engineering and Technology
Department of Chemical Engineering
E-mail: zhang-qiang@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn.
Keywords: lithium-sulfur battery, interface, carbon, host-guest chemistry, cathode.
The high energy density storage technology is strongly considered for the widely use of
portable electronic devices (e.g. smart phones, laptop computers, cameras), the development
of electric vehicles, as well as the controllable energy harvest from renewable energy (e.g.
wind energy, solar energy). Lithium–sulfur (Li-S) batteries are strongly considered due to the
fact that elemental sulfur offers a high theoretical specific capacity (1672 mAh g-1 based on
the electrochemical reaction 16 Li + S8 = 8 Li2S) and therefore, Li-S batteries demonstrate
high theoretical energy density (2600 Wh kg-1), which is 3 to 5 times higher than that of
traditional Li-ion batteries based on intercalation chemistry.[1, 2, 3] The use of sulfur as the
cathode possesses the advantages of natural abundance, low cost, environmental benignancy
and wide range of operating temperature. However, there are many basic obstacles to fully
demonstrate the potential of sulfur for a Li-S cell. Both of the sulfur and lithium sulfides are
intrinsic insulate, leading to the low utilization of active materials in the cathode and poor
electrochemical reversibility. There is a remarkable volume change during the charge-
discharge cycle. Meanwhile, the induced high order polysulfides diffuse to the anode side,
where they react with metallic lithium to form low order polysulfides.[2] The low order
polysulfides diffuse back, which is described as shuttle effect that lower the efficiency.
Consequently, the discharge capacity always decays rapidly.
The use of conductive framework as the host scaffold to obtain a carbon/sulfur
composite cathode is an efficient and effective way to increase the utilization of sulfur for
redox reaction.[1, 2] The nanostructured carbon (e.g. ordered mesoporous carbon,[4, 5]
hierarchical porous carbon,[6] carbide derived carbon,[7] carbon hollow spheres,[8] carbon
nanotubes (CNTs),[9, 10] graphene,[11] CNT/graphene hybrid,[12] CNT/porous carbon hybrid[13]),
conductive polymer (polyacrylonitrile,[14] polypyrrole,[15] polyaniline[16]), as well as
nanocarbon/polymer hybrid (polypyrrole/CNT,[17] polypyrrole/graphene,[18] polyacrylonitrile
/CNT[19]) framework was employed, they can serve as the host scaffold to accommodate the
sulfur/lithium sulfide and demonstrate significantly improvement on the capacities of the
cathodes. However, the capacity decay induced by the shuttle effect and the deterioration of
full transformation to lithium sulfides (Li2S) still remains a great challenge.
On one hand, the physical confinement of polysulfides by micropores and outer layers of
core-shell cathode structure retard the rapid diffusion of polysulfides from the carbon/sulfur
composite. The introduction of inorganic polysulfide reservoir[20] and nanocarbon
interlayer[21] are effective to absorb/desorb the polysulfides within cathodes, which hinder the
diffusion of polysulfide in a whole cell. An ion selective membrane has been employed to
block the diffusion of polysulfide anions across the membrane to the anode side,[22] which
greatly suppresses the shuttle effect in a Li-S cell. On the other hand, the introduction of solid
electrolyte[23] and lithium salt additive that tune the dissolvability/precipitation of sulfur-
containing compound can well retard the shuttle of polysulfides. LiNO3 has been applied to
obtain stable solid electrolyte interface and suppress the side reactions between polysulfides
and lithium metal.[24] The solvent-in-salt system is a good strategy to suppress the polysulfide
dissolution and the growth of lithium dendrite.[7, 25] However, when the ionic diffusion rate is
hindered, the performance of Li-S cells deteriorates. The key to improve the cycling
performance with high capacity of a Li-S cell lies in the well control of the interaction
between the conductive host framework and sulfur-containing guests (e.g. sulfur, polysulfide,
Generally, the sulfur at the interface between the conductive host and sulfur-containing
guest always offers a dynamic equilibrium during the charge-discharge cycle. A well
conductive host framework not only can well accommodate the non-polar sulfur and polar
lithium sulfides, but also intrinsically attracts the polysulfides to retard its rapid diffusion. The
topological factors of the carbon host was highly considered,[4, 5, 6, 7, 8] but the surface property,
especially the role of heteroatoms, still lacks investigation. If a sulfur crystal is employed, the
varieties of sulfur-containing guests are only dependent on the degree of discharge, and the
carbon host is the other one factor to be considered. In fact, the heteroatom incorporated into
the carbon framework renders the tunable electronic properties and hence gives rise to
interfaces anticipated to significantly improve catalytic activities[26] and electrochemical
performances.[5, 10, 27-29] The addition of nitrogen into the nanocarbon possesses unexpected
high capacity for Li batteries,[5, 10, 29, 30] supercapacitors,[28] as well as high catalytic activity
for oxygen reduction reaction[27] and dehydrogenation.[26] Based on these considerations, we
employ the idea of the use of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing nanocarbon as the conductive
host to shed a light on the role of the interface between the carbon host and sulfur guest for a
stable Li-S cell. To provide the proof-of-the-concept, the conductive carbon host made of
pristine CNTs, oxidized CNTs (OCNTs), and nitrogen doped CNTs (NCNTs) are used to
accommodate sulfur guests. The electrochemical redox reaction is depended on the interface
between the host/guest, and the nitrogen containing functional groups is expected to host the
sulfur containing guests. The cycling stability of the Li-S battery based on NCNTs is
Both the CNTs and NCNTs were mass produced via facile fluidized-bed chemical vapor
deposition (CVD). Figure 1a shows the typical morphology of CNTs as seamless tubular by
coaxially rolling multi-layered graphene. After introducing hetero nitrogen atoms into
graphitic domain, a stacked cup-like structure is available but preserves the integrity along the
c-axis (Figure 1b). The chemical oxidation of CNT using nitric acid endows the OCNTs with
increased defects and exfoliated graphene layer (Figure 1c); however, the nanocable-like
morphology is well sustained due to the mild condition of chemical oxidation. Both NCNTs
and OCNTs possess very similar adsorption behavior of type III adsorption isotherms and H3
hysteresis loops in IUPAC classification as pristine CNTs (Figure 1d). The Brunauer–
Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area of CNT, NCNT, and OCNT is 196, 233, and 196
m2 g-1, respectively. Note that the pore volume of 15-50 nm mesopores is decreased
significantly after chemical oxidation (the pore-size distribution shown as the inset figure in
Figure 1d). This suggests the abundant oxygen-functional groups on the surface of OCNTs,
which render the different stacking status due to the enhanced interaction compared with
CNTs. The XRD pattern of NCNTs shows characteristic signals of graphitic domains with
broadened peaks and decreased intensity against undoped CNTs, indicating the discontinuity
of graphene layer and the delocalized nitrogen-doped nanocaps (Figure S1).
To prepare the carbon/sulfur composite cathode material for lithium-sulfur batteries,
sulfur was infiltrated into nanocarbon via typical melting-diffusion strategy at 155 oC for 4 h
with the same initial weight ratio of carbon/sulfur. As-fabricated composites denote as CNT-S,
NCNT-S, and OCNT-S, respectively. The thermogravimetric (TG) analysis indicates the
content and the thermal stability of sulfur in carbon/sulfur composite (Figure 1e). There is no
remarkable difference on the pore size distribution and specific surface area, which determine
physically trapping ability to sulfur, among three kinds of CNTs (Figure 1d). Moreover, the
XRD pattern of well-crystalline sulfur in NCNT-S also suggests that the open-structured
interspace of NCNTs can only afford limited spatial confinement to sulfur (Figure S1).
However, the retention of sulfur in the composite is increased remarkably after surface
functionalization while the thermal stability is also promoted notably. Consequently, the
strong affinity between sulfur guests and electronically-meditated carbon host through
nitrogen-/oxygen-containing sites can be proposed as the results of enhanced interaction
potential or possibly-formed chemical bonding between sulfur and functional groups, which
was demonstrated from both theoretical and experimental results previously.[31] Hence,
cathode material with high sulfur content of NCNT-S (65 wt %) and OCNT-S (68 wt %) can
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is employed to investigate the surface
properties of CNTs, NCNTs, and OCNTs (Figure 1f and S2). Nitrogen peak is only observed
in spectrum of NCNTs, suggesting the incorporation of nitrogen into the π-delocalization of
sp2-carbon. With assistance of ammonia during CVD, the content of nitrogen in NCNT
reaches 1.76 at %. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of NCNT exhibits different
heterogeneity of nitrogen-doping forms, which is deconvoluted into three predominant
characteristic peaks of pyridinic nitrogen, quaternary nitrogen (nitrogen atoms coordinated in
the graphene lattice in substitution of carbon atoms), and chemisorbed nitrogen [32], which
have been proved to tune the distribution of electron on the carbon surface significantly
(Figure 1g). By conducting fine scanning of O1s region, distinctly different surface properties
of OCNTs against CNTs and NCNTs are detected (Figure S3). Due to the chemical oxidation
by nitric acid, OCNTs are functionalized with large quantities of oxygen-containing groups
with similar species such as hydroxyls, carbonyls, and ether groups except the greatly
With rationally designing and modulating the surface chemistry of nanocarbons, the
electrochemical behavior is dramatically facilitated. The CNT-S cathode delivers an initial
discharge capacity of 899 mAh g-1 at a current density of 1 C, while the NCNT-S and OCNT-
S cathode with even higher content of sulfur exhibit compatible capacity of 937 and 838 mAh
g-1 (Figure 2a). However, the cycling stability is significantly different. The NCNT-S cathode
exhibits the most favorable lifespan of nearly 70 % of capacity retention (645 mAh g-1) after
200 cycles, while the capacity of CNT-S and OCNT-S decay rapidly to 640 mAh g-1 during
the first 30 and 40 cycles respectively. However, the tendency of capacity fading inverses
dramatically with further cycling. The CNT-S cathode maintains the capacity of 450 mAh g-1
until cycling for 200 cycles while the OCNT-S still suffers severe degradation and only less
than 200 mAh g-1 can be available. As a result, the cyclic fading rate of initial capacity is 0.16,
0.25 and 0.38 % during 200 cycles when NCNT-S, CNT-S, and OCNT-S cathode were
introduced, respectively. Furthermore, the coulombic efficiency of NCNT-S cathode
maintains over 99.2 % during long-term cycling, demonstrating the high reversibility and
energy efficiency. In contrast, CNT-S cathode possesses lower coulombic efficiency of
94.8 % while the coulombic efficiency of OCNT-S cathode declined from nearly 100 % to
90.2 %, revealing the limited affinity between sulfur/CNT and unstable carbon-host/sulfur-
containing-guest interfaces of OCNT-S.
The galvanostatic discharge-charge curves show more details about capacity decay
(Figure 2b and S4). All of the curves exhibit typical two-plateaus profiles corresponding to
the multi-step transformation from sulfur to lithium sulfides/disulfides (Li2S/Li2S2), of which
the NCNT-S cathode possesses mitigated polarization and less shrunk plateaus of 2.0-2.4 V
(Qhigh) and 1.8-2.0 V (Qlow) during cycling while the tendency of CNT-S and OCNT-S being
extremely different. Such differences shed a light on the mechanism of capacity fading.
Generally, the discharge capacity of a multi-electron-transfer process is determined by the
active mass participating in electrochemical reactions and the number of electron transfer.
Considering two-step reaction as following (1-2), the capacity contribution of Qhigh and Qlow is
S + 0.5 Li+ + 0.5 e- → 0.25 Li2S4 (Qhigh)
0.25 Li2S4 + (x – 0.5) Li+ + (x - 0.5) e- → LixS (Qlow)
In the typical discharge curve of lithium-sulfur battery, Qhigh corresponding to the
reduction of elemental sulfur into high-order polysulfides (Li2Sx, x = 4-8) indicates the
participation of active mass. Considering that the liquid-to-solid transformation of Li2S4 to
Li2S/Li2S2 is kinetically inert and slow, Qlow is limited by the depth of lithiation except for the
utilization of sulfur. Thus, the deterioration of capacity induced by the loss of active mass and
decreased depth of lithiation is elaborated in Figure 2c, d, respectively. Firstly, the degree of
participation of sulfur is determined as ratio of Qhigh to the theoretical capacity of reaction
from sulfur to Li2S4 (418 mAh g-1) and the relative degree of participation of active mass is
normalized to initial degree of participation. As Figure 2c suggesting, chemically modified
CNT hosts, namely NCNTs and OCNTs, possess higher retention of active mass than raw
CNTs, which is ascribed to the strong interaction between sulfur molecules and surface
functional groups. Note that the retention of OCNTs becomes even worse than CNTs,
suggesting the occupation of carbon surface by inactive Li2S. Secondly, the degree of
lithiation is identified as x in the mixture product of LixS. By employing the Faraday’s Law,
the capacity is related to the transfer number of electron or the charge quantity as (3). Thus,
the degree of lithiation can be calculated as (4).
The effect of active mass losing to Qlow is eliminated. Figure 2d indicates that the
NCNT-S exhibits excellent and stable lithiation degree around 1.55 while the lithiation degree
of the CNT-S slumps from 1.59 to 1.37. A huge decrease of lithiation degree to 0.88 is
observed for OCNT-S cathode, responding to its even faster fading after 100 cycles. This is
attributed to the lower conductivity of OCNT (381 S m-1) than CNT (1185 S m-1) and NCNT
(798 S m-1), which strictly blocks the transformation from Li2S4 to full lithiated Li2S. Thus,
the NCNT-S cathode exhibits an excellent and highly reversible rate performance of 1370,
1185, 1087, and 964 mAh g-1 at current density of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 C respectively (Figure
2e). Furthermore, no significant decline of capacity was observed when the current density
The cyclic voltammogram (CV) profiles are measured to interpret the electrochemical
behavior for carbon/sulfur cathode with different interfaces (Figure 3a-c). Two pairs of
distinct redox peaks are observed and stabilize after the first cycle, in which the cathodic
peaks around 2.40-2.43 V and the anodic peaks around 2.53-2.59 V are assigned to the
transition between sulfur and high-order polysulfides (Li2Sx, x = 6-8) while the cathodic peaks
around 1.83-1.92 V and the anodic peaks around 2.38-2.45 V correspond on the
transformation between Li2S4 and solid Li2S/Li2S2. The NCNT-S cathode exhibits relatively
suppressed polarization, high intensities and almost overlapped positions of redox peaks,
demonstrating high reversibility, favorable electrode dynamics and good stability (Figure 3b).
In contrast, the CNT-S suffers unfavorable polarization and lower reversibility due to the lack
of functional groups for enhanced interaction between sulfur guests and carbon hosts (Figure
3a). Although the OCNT-S shows ameliorative reversibility especially for sulfur/high-order
polysulfides redox couple, its low conductivity renders unstable and inferior lithiation process
as the weak cathodic peaks at 1.83 V indicates (Figure 3c). In addition, the electrochemical
impedance spectra (EIS) and related equivalent circuit disclose such differences, in which the
semi-circle can be deconvoluted into two semi-circle located in high-frequency and middle-
frequency region, corresponding to the impedance of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) films
(Rf) and charge transfer (Rct) respectively (Figure 3d). The NCNT-S and OCNT-S possess
higher Rf (57.18 and 45.87 Ω) than CNT-S (39.82 Ω), suggesting severer corrosion of lithium
anodes and SEI films induced by soluble polysulfide without valid adsorption from CNT
based cathode (Table S3). However, the Rct of OCNT-S cathode (234.1 Ω) is significantly
larger than CNT-S (48.69 Ω) and NCNT-S (65.81 Ω). Such inferior charge transfer properties
results in the limited discharge depth and continuous capacity fading of OCNT-S cathode.
The role of interface between carbon host and sulfur-containing guest for the
electrochemical Li ion storage performance is proposed as follows (Figure 4). Firstly, the
oxygen-containing functional groups, especially hydroxyls and carbonylic/carboxylic groups
introduced by chemical oxidation or as concomitants of nitrogen-doping, play as an important
role for immobilizing elemental sulfur guest by sulfur-carbon and sulfur-oxygen bonding
generated in melting procedure,[31] which was demonstrated by TG results of carbon/sulfur
cathode material from the side (Figure 1e). Thus, a good distribution of sulfur in the whole
matrix is expected during the repeated dissolution/deposition in discharge/charge process.
Secondly, polysulfide anions tend to be adsorbed by positive charged quaternary nitrogen
atoms embedded in the carbon lattice by the electrostatic interaction. Similarly, carbonyl
groups neighboring the pyridinic nitrogen atoms exhibit strong affinity for anchoring
polysulfide species than neat carbon surface.[33, 34] Hence, effective chemical gradient is built
by these appropriate nitrogen-containing functional groups for trapping the polysulfides,[35]
resulting in higher Qhigh retention and coulombic efficiency of NCNTs and OCNTs than
CNTs (Figure 3a, c). Thirdly, the dominated pyridinic nitrogen is beneficial for the
generation and distribution of insulative Li2S since the nitrogen atoms conjugated in the π-
system provide extra lone pairs in the inplane sp2 orbitals for facilitating the inert
transformation to Li2S as Lewis-basic catalyst sites. Similar electrocatalyzing effects of
nitrogen-doped nanocarbon have been investigated in other multi-electron-transfer process
such as oxygen reduction reactions and fuel cells.[27] Both CNTs and OCNTs lack such ability,
hence exhibit much lower degree of lithiation and poorer rate performance than the NCNTs
(Figure 3d). Furthermore, such electron-modulated surface similar to amphiphilic polymer or
carbonized polyacrylonitrile also effectively anchors the Li2S as polysulfide, leading to good
distribution and immobilization.[33, 36] Further investigation are required to reveal the rational
combination of the benefiting effects towards a Li-S cell with high capacity and stable
In summary, a novel strategy towards to high-performance CNT-sulfur cathode material
by building strongly-coupled interface between surface-mediated carbon hosts and various
sulfur-containing guests was developed for highly-stable lithium-sulfur batteries. Compared
to CNTs and OCNTs, NCNTs with nitrogen heterogenization were employed as promising
conductive host to accommodate sulfur guest with high content of 65 wt % (58.5 wt % in the
whole cathode), exhibiting a highly reversible, efficient and stable Li storage properties such
as mitigated polarization, high capacity of 1370 and 964 mAh g-1 at 0.1 C and 1 C and 70 %
of capacity retention after 200 cycles. The outstanding reversibility and stability of NCNT-S
cathode is attributed to the favorable guest-host interaction at the nitrogen-doped
carbon/sulfur interface, manifesting as (1) good accommodation of elemental sulfur with
benign sulfur-carbon and sulfur-oxygen affinity, (2) chemical gradient to adsorb polysulfide
anions by positive charged quaternary nitrogen atoms as well as the carbonyl groups
neighboring the pyridinic nitrogen atoms, and (3) ameliorative deposition and distribution of
Li2S on the dominated pyridinic nitrogen area. This not only provides mechanistic energy
chemistry to prepare a well mediated carbon host/sulfur guest cathode, but also embodies the
use of nanomaterials for Li ion storage with high capacity and good cyclability. The further
investigation on hierarchical composite electrodes based on the well rational combination of
conductive sp2 nanocarbon/polymer host with loading guests are highly required to provide
the optimized interfaces with high activity and good stability for Li-S cells, Li-air batteries,
Li-ion batteries, supercapacitors, and solar cells.
Supporting Information is available from the Wiley Online Library or from the author.
We thank helpful discussion with Prof. Feng Li, Guangmin Zhou, and Lin Zhu. This work

China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2012M520293, 2013T60125).

H. J. Peng, Prof. Q. Zhang, Dr. J. Q. Huang, X. B. Huang, M. Q. Guo, Z. Yuan, L. Y. He,
Strongly Coupled Interfaces between Carbon Host and Sulfur-Containing Guest for
Highly-Stable Lithium-Sulfur Batteries
Rational combination of conductive hosts with sulfur-containing guests: The sulfur is well
accommodated by sulfur-carbon and sulfur-oxygen affinity, the polysulfide anions tend to be
adsorbed, and the insulative Li2S is inclined to deposit on the dominated pyridinic-nitrogen
area. Therefore, the nitrogen doped carbon exhibits remarkably improved lithium storage
performance for lithium-sulfur battery.

Synthesis of CNT, NCNT, and OCNT: The synthesis of CNT/NCNT was carried out by a
previously-reported catalytic CVD in fluidized bed with/without ammonia assistance using
FeMgAl layered double hydroxide as catalysts and ethylene as carbon sources. The OCNT
was fabricated by a facile chemical oxidation of raw CNTs by nitric acid of 7.5 mol L-1 for 30
Fabrication of carbon/sulfur cathode material: The carbon/sulfur cathode materials were
fabricated with a typical melt-diffusion strategy. The nanocarbon were firstly mixed with
sulfur powder with a mass ratio of 3 : 7 by milling. The mixture was subsequently placed in a
sealed flask at 155 oC for 2 h to incorporate sulfur into the carbonaceous matrix.
Characterizations: The morphology of the samples were characterized by a JEM 2010 (JEOL
Ltd, Tokyo, Japan) TEM operated at 120.0 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were
recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer at 40.0 kV and 120 mA with Cu-Kα
radiation. The pore-size distribution and BET specific surface area of the samples were
measured by N2 adsorption/desorption using Autosorb-IQ2-MP-C system. The pore size
distribution and pore volume of the nanocarbon samples were calculated by the quenched
solid state functional theory (QSDFT) method. The TG analysis (TGA) was carried out on the
samples using TGA/DSC1 STARe system under N2 atmosphere. The conductivity
measurements of nanocarbon disk pre-compressed at 5 MPa were performed using the KDY-
Electrochemical measurements: Two-electrode cells using standard 2025 coil-type cells were
constructed to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the cathode materials for lithium-
sulfur batteries. The cathode slurry was prepared by mixing 90 % of the carbon/sulfur
materials and 10 % of the poly(vinylidene fluoride) binder in a N-methyl-pyrrolidone (NMP)
solvent dispersant. The positive electrodes were fabricated by coating the slurry on aluminum
foil and drying at 60 oC for 12 hr. A 1 mol L-1 lithium trifluoromethanesulfonate (LiCF3SO3)
and 0.25 mol L-1 lithium nitrate (LiNO3) solution in tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether
(TEGDME) was used for electrochemical evaluation. Lithium metal foil was used as the
anode and the polypropylene membranes from Celgard Inc. were used as the separators. The
coin cells were tested in galvanostatic mode at various currents within a voltage range of 1.7 -
2.8 V using Neware multichannel battery cycler. The CV and EIS measurements were
performed on Solartron 1470E electrochemical workstation at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s-1. A
current density of 1672 mA g-1 (1 C) equivalent to full discharge or charge in one hour was
applied in both current sweep directions. The capacities were calculated based on the mass of
